assertiveness_why: Prepared questions with excellent technical follow-ups; occasional direct challenges (on human connection concerns, Facebook acquisition rationale) but primarily receiving and exploring Noam's expertise rather than asserting contrary positions.
question_style: Open-ended exploration of technical and philosophical territory; probing questions on compute constraints, model architecture, and product strategy; occasional light challenges (e.g., Facebook pivot, hallucinations-as-feature) but collaborative rather than adversarial.
tone: Warm and engaged with deep respect for technical expertise; self-deprecating humor (tedious tasks quip); willing to venture into unexpected personal territory (parenthood, religion, sleep) without fandom or deference.
depth: Exceptional technical depth: language modeling fundamentals, training compute requirements ($2M for current model), sparse vs dense computation, mixture-of-experts architecture, data vs model-size tradeoffs, hardware constraints on neural networks, and research publication quality challenges.
harry_quotes: Do you not worry that we're losing touch with other humans?
Why is character a standalone company and not a product of Facebook?
Is character an education company? Is it a social company?
What do you think society believes that you wish they'd change?
learnings: Language models trained at scale become general-purpose tools that discover use cases users never anticipated; the best applications haven't been invented yet.
Compute spent during training—not just data size or parameter count—is the primary constraint on model capability and performance.
Startups outpace big tech on AI product launches because they face less organizational friction and brand-risk concerns slowing deployment.
Hallucinations in language models can be features for entertainment and emotional-support applications, not inherent bugs to eliminate.
Multiple winners will emerge: big companies leveraging scale and distribution, startups moving fast and iterating on consumer use cases, and individuals accessing increasingly accessible hardware.
Open-source research accelerates community progress even when large companies close proprietary research; economics of scale favor big-company serving but experimentation favors openness.
notable: Harry ventures into unexpected personal territory—parenthood, religious philosophy, sleep deprivation—in the context of AI development and Noam's career choices. Noam engages thoughtfully despite the atypical direction; this signals Harry experimenting with psychological/lifestyle angles in technical interviews.
